weeks.title: Week 1 — Linux, Networking & Cloud Basics
weeks.goal: Build the foundation: understand how machines talk to each other, how the internet actually works, and set up your AWS account. You already use these daily — now understand why they work.

days.title: Linux Fundamentals Refresher

blocks.content: Review Linux process model: what is a process vs thread, how Node.js uses a single process with event loop. Understand file descriptors, stdin/stdout/stderr. These concepts underpin containers and cloud VMs.
SSH into a machine (use your local terminal or spin up an AWS EC2 free-tier instance). Practice: ps aux, top/htop, netstat -tlnp, df -h, lsof. Understand what each column means.
Practice systemd: create a simple service file for a Node.js app. Start/stop/restart it. Check logs with journalctl. Understand how production apps are managed on servers.
Read DDIA Chapter 1 (Reliable, Scalable, Maintainable Applications). Take notes on what each term means. This chapter frames everything that follows.

days.deliverable: A running EC2 instance with SSH access and a Node.js app managed by systemd

days.title: Networking — DNS, TCP/IP, HTTP Deep Dive

blocks.content: Understand the OSI model layers that matter: Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) and Layer 7 (HTTP). Learn what happens when you type a URL: DNS resolution → TCP handshake → TLS handshake → HTTP request → response.
Deep dive into DNS: A records, CNAME, NS records, TTL. How DNS propagation works. What Route 53 does. Draw the full DNS resolution flow on paper.
Use dig, nslookup, traceroute, curl -v to trace actual network paths. Run dig google.com and understand every line of output. Use curl -v on your own API to see headers.
Learn HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3. Understand keep-alive connections, head-of-line blocking, multiplexing. Learn what a reverse proxy does (Nginx) and why it matters for Node.js.

days.deliverable: Hand-drawn diagram of the full journey of an HTTP request, from browser to server and back

days.title: Load Balancers & Reverse Proxies

blocks.content: Understand Layer 4 vs Layer 7 load balancers. L4 routes by IP/port (fast, dumb). L7 routes by URL path, headers, cookies (smart, slower). When to use each.

Load balancing algorithms: Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hash (sticky sessions), Weighted Round Robin. Draw each one. Understand when sticky sessions are needed (stateful apps) and why they're generally bad.
Set up Nginx as a reverse proxy on your EC2 instance. Configure it to proxy requests to your Node.js app. Add a second Node instance on a different port. Configure Nginx to load balance between them.
Add health checks in Nginx config. Test: kill one Node process and verify Nginx routes to the healthy one. Install and use ab (Apache Bench) or wrk to generate load and observe distribution.

days.deliverable: Nginx config load balancing between 2 Node.js instances with health checks

days.title: AWS Core — IAM, VPC, EC2

blocks.content: Create a proper AWS account (if not done). Set up IAM: create an admin user (never use root), enable MFA, create a policy that allows only specific actions. Understand IAM roles vs users vs policies.
Learn VPC: what is a VPC, subnets (public vs private), internet gateway, NAT gateway, route tables, security groups vs NACLs. Create a VPC from scratch (don't use default).
Launch an EC2 instance inside your custom VPC's public subnet. Configure security group to allow SSH (port 22) and HTTP (port 80) only. SSH in and deploy your Node app.

Create a private subnet. Try to understand why a database should live here (no public internet access). Set up a NAT gateway so the private subnet can still pull packages. This is real-world VPC architecture.

days.deliverable: Custom VPC with public + private subnets, EC2 running in public subnet, security groups configured

days.title: AWS Storage — S3, EBS, and Data Tiers

blocks.content: Understand storage types: Block storage (EBS — like a hard drive attached to EC2), Object storage (S3 — for files, images, backups), File storage (EFS — shared filesystem). When to use each.
Create an S3 bucket. Upload files via CLI (aws s3 cp). Configure bucket policies for public read access on a specific folder. Set up lifecycle rules to move old objects to Glacier after 90 days.
Modify your Node.js app to upload images to S3 using the AWS SDK. Generate presigned URLs for secure temporary access. This is how most production apps handle file uploads.

Read about S3 internals: how S3 achieves 99.999999999% durability (11 nines). Understand eventual consistency in S3 (now strong consistency for PUTs). Learn about S3 storage classes and cost optimization.

days.deliverable: Node.js app uploading files to S3 with presigned URLs

days.title: AWS Databases — RDS & DynamoDB Intro

blocks.content: Understand RDS: managed relational DB. What AWS handles for you (patching, backups, failover) vs what you handle (schema, queries, indexes). Multi-AZ deployment for high availability.
Launch a MySQL RDS instance in your private subnet. Connect to it from your EC2 instance. Migrate your existing MySQL schema and data. Verify your Node app works with RDS instead of local MySQL.

Introduction to DynamoDB: key-value + document store. Understand partition keys, sort keys, read/write capacity units. When to choose DynamoDB over RDS (high scale, simple access patterns, low latency).
Create a DynamoDB table. Write a small Node.js script to do CRUD operations using the AWS SDK. Experiment with queries vs scans. Understand why scans are expensive.

days.deliverable: Node app connected to RDS MySQL + a DynamoDB CRUD script

days.title: Week 1 Review & Mini-Project

blocks.content: Review all notes from the week. Draw the complete architecture of what you've built: Client → Nginx LB → EC2 (Node.js) → RDS (MySQL) + S3 (files). Label every component.
Build a simple image gallery API that ties everything together: Node.js API on EC2 behind Nginx, images stored in S3, metadata in RDS, served via presigned URLs. Deploy it properly on your VPC.

Answer without looking: What's the difference between security groups and NACLs? Why use a private subnet for RDS? What happens if the Nginx process crashes? How does S3 achieve 11 nines durability? What's the difference between L4 and L7 load balancing?

days.deliverable: Deployed image gallery API with architecture diagram

weeks.title: Week 2 — Scaling, Caching & CDNs
weeks.goal: Learn how to make systems fast and handle millions of users. Understand horizontal scaling, caching at every layer, and content delivery.

days.title: Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling

blocks.content: Understand vertical scaling (bigger machine) vs horizontal scaling (more machines). Why vertical has a ceiling. The challenges horizontal introduces: data consistency, session management, service discovery.
Read DDIA Chapter 2 (Data Models and Query Languages). Understand relational vs document vs graph models. Think about which fits your past projects.

Study stateless vs stateful services. Your Node.js app with sessions stored in-memory is stateful — how to make it stateless: move sessions to Redis/DB. Refactor your app to use Redis for session storage.
Use AWS Auto Scaling Groups: create a launch template from your EC2 instance. Set up an ASG with min=1, max=3. Put an Application Load Balancer (ALB) in front. Test that new instances auto-register.

days.deliverable: Auto Scaling Group with ALB, stateless Node.js app with Redis sessions

days.title: Caching Fundamentals — Redis Deep Dive

blocks.content: Why caching works: locality of reference. Understand the caching layers: browser cache, CDN cache, application cache (Redis/Memcached), database cache (query cache, buffer pool). Each has different TTLs and invalidation strategies.

Cache strategies: Cache-Aside (app checks cache first, loads from DB on miss), Write-Through (write to cache and DB simultaneously), Write-Behind (write to cache, async flush to DB), Read-Through. Draw each pattern.

Set up ElastiCache Redis on AWS (or local Redis). Implement cache-aside pattern in your Node app: check Redis first, if miss → query MySQL → store result in Redis with TTL. Measure response time before/after.

Cache eviction policies: LRU (least recently used — the default), LFU (least frequently used), TTL-based. The cache stampede problem: when a hot key expires and 1000 requests simultaneously hit the DB. Solutions: locking, staggered TTL.

days.deliverable: Cache-aside implementation in Node.js with before/after latency comparison

days.title: CDN & Edge Computing

blocks.content: How a CDN works: origin server → edge locations worldwide. Understand pull-based (edge fetches from origin on first request) vs push-based (you upload to all edges). Cache-Control headers, max-age, s-maxage, stale-while-revalidate.
Set up CloudFront in front of your S3 bucket. Serve images through CloudFront. Test latency from different regions using online tools. Configure cache behaviors for different file types.
Learn about cache invalidation in CDNs: why it's called one of the two hardest problems in CS. Strategies: versioned URLs (style.v2.css), cache-busting query strings, explicit invalidation API calls.

Introduction to edge computing: CloudFront Functions, Lambda@Edge. The idea of running code at the edge (authentication, URL rewriting, A/B testing). Understand when edge makes sense vs when it doesn't.

days.deliverable: CloudFront distribution serving S3 assets with proper cache headers

days.title: Database Indexing & Query Optimization

blocks.content: How database indexes work: B-Tree indexes (most common — good for range queries), Hash indexes (exact match only, very fast). Understand that every index speeds up reads but slows down writes.
Read DDIA Chapter 3 (Storage and Retrieval). Understand LSM-Trees vs B-Trees. This is the foundation of why different databases perform differently.
In your RDS MySQL: run EXPLAIN on your queries. Add indexes and see how the query plan changes. Practice creating composite indexes. Understand the leftmost prefix rule.

Generate 1 million rows of test data using a script. Run queries with and without indexes, measure the time difference. Practice slow query log analysis. This is a skill you'll use weekly in real jobs.

days.deliverable: Query optimization report: before/after EXPLAIN plans with index improvements

days.title: Database Replication — Read Replicas

blocks.content: Read DDIA Chapter 5 (Replication). Understand leader-follower replication: one leader handles writes, followers replicate and handle reads. Synchronous vs asynchronous replication.

Problems with replication: replication lag (follower is behind leader), read-after-write consistency (user writes then reads from replica, doesn't see their own write), monotonic reads. Solutions to each.
Create a read replica of your RDS instance. Modify your Node.js app to send writes to the primary and reads to the replica. Use separate connection pools.

Multi-leader replication: when and why (multi-datacenter). The conflict resolution problem: two leaders accept conflicting writes. Strategies: last-write-wins, custom merge, CRDTs (intro only for now).

days.deliverable: Node.js app with read/write splitting across primary and replica

days.title: Database Partitioning / Sharding

blocks.content: Read DDIA Chapter 6 (Partitioning). Why shard: single DB can't handle the data volume or write throughput. Horizontal partitioning (sharding) vs vertical partitioning.

Sharding strategies: Range-based (user IDs 1-1M on shard 1, 1M-2M on shard 2 — risk of hotspots), Hash-based (hash(user_id) mod N — even distribution but no range queries), Directory-based (lookup table maps key → shard).

The problems sharding creates: cross-shard queries (JOINs across shards are expensive/impossible), resharding (adding a new shard means redistributing data), hotspot handling (celebrity problem — one shard gets all traffic).
Consistent hashing: the elegant solution to the resharding problem. Draw the hash ring. Understand virtual nodes. This is asked in almost every system design interview. Practice explaining it aloud.

days.deliverable: Hand-drawn diagrams of each sharding strategy + consistent hashing ring

days.title: Week 2 Review & Caching Project

blocks.content: Review all notes. Key questions to answer: When would you use cache-aside vs write-through? What's the difference between CDN pull and push? Why is consistent hashing better than simple mod-N? What causes replication lag?
Enhance your image gallery: add Redis caching for popular images, CloudFront CDN for static assets, and read replicas for read-heavy queries. Measure the performance improvement at each step.
Practice designing a URL shortener using the 6-step framework. Focus on: how to generate short URLs, where to store the mappings, how to cache hot URLs, how to handle 301 vs 302 redirects.

days.deliverable: Full-stack image gallery with caching at every layer + URL shortener design doc

weeks.title: Week 3 — Containers, Orchestration & CI/CD
weeks.goal: Containerize everything, learn Kubernetes fundamentals, and set up a real deployment pipeline. This is how modern cloud-native apps are deployed.

days.title: Docker Deep Dive

blocks.content: How Docker works under the hood: namespaces (process isolation), cgroups (resource limits), union filesystems (layered images). The difference between a container and a VM. Why containers are faster to start.

Write a production-quality Dockerfile for your Node.js app: multi-stage build (build stage + production stage), non-root user, proper .dockerignore, small base image (node:alpine). Understand each layer.
Docker Compose: create a docker-compose.yml that runs your Node app + Redis + MySQL together. Set up networking between containers. Use volumes for database persistence. Add health checks.
Push your image to Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry). Understand image tagging strategies (latest, git SHA, semantic versioning). Pull and run from ECR on a fresh EC2 instance.

days.deliverable: Production Dockerfile + Docker Compose with 3 services + image pushed to ECR

days.title: Kubernetes — Core Concepts

blocks.content: K8s architecture: Control plane (API server, scheduler, controller manager, etcd) vs worker nodes (kubelet, kube-proxy, container runtime). How a pod gets scheduled.
Core objects: Pod (smallest unit, 1+ containers), Deployment (manages ReplicaSets for rolling updates), Service (stable network endpoint: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer), ConfigMap/Secret.
Install Minikube or use Kind (Kubernetes in Docker). Deploy your Node.js app: write a Deployment YAML (3 replicas) and a Service YAML (type: LoadBalancer). Use kubectl get pods/svc/deploy.
Practice kubectl: exec into a pod, view logs, describe resources, port-forward. Kill a pod and watch K8s restart it automatically. Scale the deployment to 5 replicas and back to 2.

days.deliverable: Node.js app running on Kubernetes with 3 replicas and a LoadBalancer service

days.title: Kubernetes — Scaling & Networking

blocks.content: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA): scales pods based on CPU/memory metrics. Vertical Pod Autoscaler: adjusts resource requests. Cluster Autoscaler: adds/removes nodes.
Set up HPA for your deployment: target 50% CPU. Install metrics-server. Generate load with a simple script and watch pods scale up. Remove load and watch them scale down.

K8s networking: every pod gets its own IP. Services provide stable endpoints via kube-proxy and iptables. Ingress: L7 load balancing with path-based or host-based routing. Install Nginx Ingress Controller.
Create an Ingress resource that routes /api to your backend service and /static to a frontend service. This is how most production K8s apps expose endpoints.

days.deliverable: HPA configured + Ingress routing to multiple services

days.title: CI/CD Pipeline

blocks.content: CI/CD concepts: Continuous Integration (every commit triggers build + test), Continuous Delivery (automated deployment to staging), Continuous Deployment (automated deployment to production). The deployment pipeline.
Set up GitHub Actions for your project: on push → run tests → build Docker image → push to ECR. Write the workflow YAML step by step. Understand runners, jobs, steps, and secrets.
Add the deployment step: after image push, update the K8s deployment with the new image tag. Use kubectl set image or apply a new manifest. Set up staging and production environments.
Add quality gates: linting (ESLint), unit tests (Jest), security scanning (npm audit, Snyk). The pipeline should block if any gate fails. Push a broken commit and verify it catches the error.

days.deliverable: Complete CI/CD pipeline: push → test → build → deploy to K8s

days.title: Infrastructure as Code — Terraform

blocks.content: Why IaC: reproducibility, version control, auditability. Terraform vs CloudFormation vs Pulumi. Terraform's model: providers (AWS, GCP), resources, state file, plan → apply workflow.
Install Terraform. Write your first config: create a VPC + subnet + EC2 instance using HCL. Run terraform init, plan, apply. Look at the state file. Run destroy.
Recreate your entire AWS infrastructure in Terraform: VPC, subnets, security groups, RDS, S3 bucket, ALB. Use variables and outputs. This replaces everything you clicked in the console.
Terraform state management: why local state is dangerous (team collaboration). Set up remote state in S3 with DynamoDB locking. Understand state drift and how to handle it.

days.deliverable: Full AWS infrastructure defined in Terraform with remote state

days.title: Observability — Logging, Metrics, Tracing

blocks.content: The three pillars of observability: Logs (what happened), Metrics (how much/how fast), Traces (the path of a request through services). Each answers different questions. You need all three.
Set up structured logging in your Node.js app using Winston or Pino. JSON format, log levels (error/warn/info/debug), correlation IDs. Send logs to CloudWatch Logs.
Set up Prometheus + Grafana locally (docker-compose). Expose /metrics from your Node app using prom-client. Create dashboards for: request rate, error rate, latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99).
Learn about distributed tracing: how a single request flows through multiple services. OpenTelemetry standard. The concept of spans and trace context propagation. RED method: Rate, Errors, Duration.

days.deliverable: Grafana dashboard with request rate, error rate, and latency charts

days.title: Week 3 Review & Full Stack Deployment

blocks.content: Review: Draw the full deployment pipeline from git push to running in K8s. Explain Docker layering, K8s self-healing, HPA scaling, Terraform state. These are interview gold.

Deploy your full-stack app the cloud-native way: Terraform provisions infrastructure → GitHub Actions builds and pushes Docker image → K8s Deployment runs the app → Prometheus/Grafana monitors it. End-to-end.
Design a notification service (push notifications, email, SMS). Focus on: how to handle different channels, retry logic for failures, user preference management, rate limiting notifications.

days.deliverable: Complete cloud-native deployment: IaC + CI/CD + K8s + monitoring

weeks.title: Week 4 — Distributed Systems Core Theory
weeks.goal: The hardest and most important week. Master the theoretical foundations: CAP theorem, consistency models, consensus, and distributed transactions. This is what separates senior engineers.

days.title: CAP Theorem & Consistency Models

blocks.content: The CAP theorem: in a network partition, you must choose between Consistency and Availability. Understand why you can't have both. Real-world: CP systems (HBase, MongoDB with majority reads) vs AP systems (Cassandra, DynamoDB).

Why CAP is oversimplified: the PACELC extension. In normal operation (no partition), you still choose between Latency and Consistency. Most real systems make different choices for different operations.

Consistency models ranked: Linearizability (strongest — behaves like a single copy), Sequential consistency, Causal consistency, Eventual consistency (weakest). Draw examples of each with timeline diagrams.

Read DDIA Chapter 7 (Transactions) first half. Understand ACID properties deeply. Focus on Isolation levels: Read Uncommitted → Read Committed → Repeatable Read → Serializable. What anomalies each prevents.

days.deliverable: Comparison chart of consistency models with real-world database examples for each

days.title: Distributed Transactions

blocks.content: Why distributed transactions are hard: the Two Generals Problem (can't guarantee agreement over unreliable network). The FLP impossibility result (no deterministic consensus in async system with even one failure).

Two-Phase Commit (2PC): coordinator sends prepare → participants vote yes/no → coordinator sends commit/abort. The problem: if coordinator crashes after prepare, participants are blocked forever. Draw the failure scenarios.

Saga pattern: the practical alternative. Break a distributed transaction into local transactions with compensating actions. Example: Order Service → Payment Service → Inventory Service. If payment fails, compensate by canceling the order.
Saga orchestration vs choreography. Orchestrator: central coordinator tells each service what to do. Choreography: each service listens to events and reacts. Pros/cons of each. Draw both patterns.

days.deliverable: Diagram of 2PC flow + Saga pattern (both orchestration and choreography) for an e-commerce order

days.title: Consensus — Raft Protocol

blocks.content: Why consensus matters: multiple nodes need to agree on a value even if some nodes crash. This is the foundation of distributed databases, leader election, and configuration services.

Raft protocol: leader election (term numbers, random timeouts), log replication (leader appends entries, followers replicate), safety (only candidates with all committed entries can become leader). Watch the Raft visualization at raft.github.io.
How Raft handles failures: leader crash (new election), follower crash (leader retries), network partition (old leader steps down when it sees a higher term). Walk through each scenario step by step.

Real-world consensus: etcd (uses Raft — powers Kubernetes), ZooKeeper (uses ZAB protocol — similar to Raft), Consul (uses Raft for service discovery). You don't implement Raft, but you must understand it for interviews.

days.deliverable: Step-by-step walkthrough of Raft leader election and log replication on paper

days.title: Message Queues & Event-Driven Architecture

blocks.content: Why async: decoupling producers from consumers, buffering spikes, enabling retries. Message queue (SQS, RabbitMQ — each message consumed by one consumer) vs event stream (Kafka, Kinesis — messages consumed by many consumers, replayable).

Delivery guarantees: at-most-once (fire and forget), at-least-once (retry until ack — may duplicate), exactly-once (very hard — usually at-least-once + idempotency). Most systems use at-least-once with idempotent consumers.
Set up AWS SQS. Create a queue. Write a Node.js producer that sends messages and a consumer that processes them. Implement visibility timeout and dead-letter queue for failed messages.
Kafka fundamentals: topics, partitions, consumer groups, offsets. How Kafka achieves high throughput (sequential disk I/O, zero-copy). When to use Kafka vs SQS. Event sourcing pattern introduction.

days.deliverable: SQS producer/consumer in Node.js with dead-letter queue handling

days.title: Kafka Deep Dive & Event Sourcing

blocks.content: Kafka architecture: brokers, partitions (parallelism unit), replication factor (durability), consumer groups (each partition consumed by exactly one consumer in a group). Draw the full picture.

Run Kafka locally with Docker Compose (use Confluent images or Redpanda as a lighter alternative). Create a topic with 3 partitions. Write a Node.js producer using kafkajs. Send 1000 messages and observe partition distribution.
Write a consumer group with 3 consumers. Verify each partition goes to one consumer. Kill a consumer and verify rebalancing. This is exactly how production Kafka consumers work.

Event Sourcing: instead of storing current state, store the sequence of events. The current state is derived by replaying events. Benefits: complete audit trail, temporal queries, rebuild state. CQRS: separate read and write models.

days.deliverable: Kafka producer + consumer group in Node.js demonstrating partition rebalancing

days.title: Distributed Systems Patterns

blocks.content: Circuit Breaker pattern: prevent cascading failures. States: Closed (normal) → Open (failing, don't call) → Half-Open (test one request). Like a real circuit breaker in your house. Used in Netflix Hystrix, resilience4j.

Retry with exponential backoff: retry failed requests with increasing delays (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s...) + jitter (random offset to prevent thundering herd). Idempotency: design operations so retrying is safe. Idempotency keys.

Bulkhead pattern: isolate resources so one failing component doesn't consume all resources. Like compartments in a ship. Thread pool isolation, connection pool isolation. Rate limiting (you already designed this!).

Service discovery: how do services find each other? Client-side discovery (client queries registry) vs server-side discovery (load balancer queries registry). DNS-based (simple) vs dedicated registry (Consul, etcd, K8s Service).

days.deliverable: Cheat sheet of all distributed system patterns with when to use each

days.title: Week 4 Review & System Design Practice

blocks.content: This was the theory-heavy week. Ensure you can explain: CAP theorem (and why it's nuanced), eventual vs strong consistency, how Raft leader election works, saga vs 2PC, Kafka partitions and consumer groups, circuit breaker pattern.
Design a chat system (WhatsApp). Apply this week's knowledge: WebSockets for real-time, Kafka for message delivery, read receipts as events, group chat fan-out, message storage and retrieval.
Design a distributed task queue (like Celery/Sidekiq). How to assign tasks, handle worker failures, ensure at-least-once processing, dead-letter handling, priority queues, rate limiting per tenant.
Read DDIA Chapter 8 (The Trouble with Distributed Systems). This chapter covers everything that can go wrong: unreliable networks, unreliable clocks, process pauses. Sobering but essential.

days.deliverable: WhatsApp system design doc + distributed task queue design doc

weeks.title: Week 5 — Microservices, APIs & Advanced Cloud
weeks.goal: Learn how to break a monolith into microservices, design clean APIs, and use advanced AWS services. This is the architecture week.

days.title: Microservices Architecture

blocks.content: Monolith vs microservices: monolith is simpler to develop/deploy/debug. Microservices offer independent scaling, deployment, and technology choice. The key question: when to split? Answer: when team size and domain complexity demand it.

Domain-Driven Design (DDD) basics: bounded contexts, aggregates, domain events. Each microservice should align with a bounded context. Example: an e-commerce system has Order context, Payment context, Inventory context.

Communication patterns: Synchronous (REST, gRPC — simple but creates coupling) vs Asynchronous (events via Kafka/SQS — decoupled but harder to debug). When to use each. The rule: sync for queries, async for commands/events.

The challenges: distributed data management (each service owns its DB), service mesh (Istio/Envoy for observability, retries, mTLS), API gateway pattern (single entry point, aggregation, auth). Draw the complete microservices topology.

days.deliverable: Architecture diagram of an e-commerce system split into microservices with communication patterns labeled

days.title: API Design — REST & gRPC

blocks.content: GraphQL intro: client specifies exactly what fields it needs. Solves over-fetching and under-fetching. N+1 problem and DataLoader pattern. When GraphQL makes sense (complex frontend with many entities) vs when REST is better.

days.deliverable: Refactored REST API with pagination, versioning, and proper error handling

days.title: Serverless — Lambda & Event-Driven

blocks.content: Serverless patterns: fan-out/fan-in (one event triggers many Lambdas), Step Functions (orchestrate multi-step workflows), event sourcing with Lambda. Limitations: 15-minute timeout, 10GB memory max, no persistent connections.

days.deliverable: Serverless image processing pipeline: S3 → Lambda → S3 + DynamoDB

days.title: Authentication & Security

blocks.content: Cloud security: principle of least privilege (IAM policies), encryption at rest (KMS), encryption in transit (TLS everywhere), VPC security (private subnets for databases, security groups as firewalls). The shared responsibility model.

days.deliverable: JWT auth with refresh token rotation + secrets stored in AWS Secrets Manager

days.title: Microservices Project — Part 1

blocks.content: Build the Order Service: create order (calls Product Service to validate items, calls User Service to validate user). Use SQS to notify other services of order events asynchronously.

days.deliverable: Three microservices with separate DBs, communicating via REST + SQS

days.title: Microservices Project — Part 2 (Deploy)

blocks.content: Test failure scenarios: kill the Product Service pod — does the Order Service handle it gracefully? Implement a circuit breaker in the Order Service using a library like opossum. Add retry logic with exponential backoff.

days.deliverable: 3 microservices on K8s with monitoring, circuit breakers, and resilience testing

days.title: Week 5 Review & System Design

blocks.content: Read DDIA Chapter 11 (Stream Processing). Understand event streams, change data capture (CDC), stream joins, and how to process events in real-time. This ties together Kafka + microservices.

days.deliverable: Twitter + YouTube system design docs

weeks.title: Week 6 — Advanced Distributed Systems
weeks.goal: Go deeper: distributed storage, search systems, time & ordering, and advanced scaling patterns. This is senior/staff-level material.

days.title: Distributed Storage — How Databases Scale

blocks.content: Gossip protocol: how nodes discover each other and share state without a central coordinator. Used in Cassandra for cluster membership. Epidemic-style information spread. Eventually consistent but resilient.

days.deliverable: Diagram of Dynamo-style replication with quorum reads/writes and conflict resolution

days.title: Search Systems — Elasticsearch

blocks.content: How Elasticsearch scales: shards (partitions of an index), replicas (copies of each shard). How a search query is distributed across shards and results are merged. The relevance scoring pipeline.

days.deliverable: Elasticsearch search + autocomplete integrated into the Product Service

days.title: Time & Ordering in Distributed Systems

blocks.content: Distributed ID generation: why auto-increment doesn't work across shards. Solutions: UUID (simple but large and unsortable), Snowflake IDs (timestamp + machine ID + sequence — used by Twitter), ULID (sortable UUIDs). Implement a Snowflake generator.

days.deliverable: Snowflake ID generator implementation + vector clock walkthrough on paper

days.title: Advanced Caching & Data Structures

blocks.content: Write-ahead log (WAL): how databases ensure durability. Every write goes to the log first, then to the actual data structure. Crash recovery by replaying the log. Used in PostgreSQL, MySQL, Kafka, etcd.

days.deliverable: Redis-based trending topics system + implementation of Bloom filter concept

days.title: Data Pipeline & Batch Processing

blocks.content: Change Data Capture (CDC): capture every change in a database and publish as events. Tools: Debezium (reads MySQL/PostgreSQL binlog). Use case: keep Elasticsearch in sync with your database without dual writes.

days.deliverable: Architecture diagram comparing Lambda vs Kappa architectures

days.title: Designing for Failure

blocks.content: Disaster recovery: RPO (Recovery Point Objective — how much data can you lose?) and RTO (Recovery Time Objective — how long can you be down?). Strategies: backup/restore (hours), pilot light (minutes), warm standby (seconds), multi-site active-active (near zero).

days.deliverable: Disaster recovery plan for your microservices project with RPO/RTO targets

days.title: Week 6 Review & System Design Marathon

blocks.content: Design a distributed cache (like Memcached/Redis): consistent hashing for key distribution, replication for HA, eviction policies, hot key handling, cache client with failover.

days.deliverable: Google Drive + Web Crawler + Distributed Cache design docs

weeks.title: Week 7 — Real-World Architecture & Patterns
weeks.goal: Study how real companies build systems. Learn architectural patterns that come up in every interview. Build your pattern recognition.

days.title: Real-World Case Study — Netflix

blocks.content: Netflix's reliability practices: Chaos Monkey, regional failover (active-active in 3 AWS regions), stateless services, eventual consistency tradeoffs. How they handle the Monday night traffic spike.

days.deliverable: Architecture diagram of Netflix's full system from a user pressing play to video appearing

days.title: Real-World Case Study — Uber & WhatsApp

blocks.content: Common patterns across all three: connection management at scale (WebSockets, long polling), geospatial indexing, real-time pub/sub, offline-first design. How these patterns apply to any system.

days.deliverable: Notes comparing architectural decisions across Netflix, Uber, and WhatsApp

days.title: Database Selection Guide

blocks.content: The polyglot persistence pattern: use different databases for different parts of your system. E-commerce example: PostgreSQL for orders, Redis for sessions, Elasticsearch for search, Cassandra for activity logs. Draw the complete data architecture.

days.deliverable: Database decision matrix: requirements → database choice with justification

days.title: API Gateway & Service Mesh Deep Dive

blocks.content: When you need a service mesh vs when it's overkill. For <10 services, an API gateway + manual resilience patterns suffice. For 50+ services with complex networking requirements, a service mesh saves engineering time.

days.deliverable: API Gateway configured for microservices project with rate limiting and auth

days.title: CQRS & Event Sourcing Project

blocks.content: Test the full flow: open account → deposit → withdraw → query balance. Verify that replaying all events from the beginning reconstructs the current state. Add a snapshot mechanism for performance.

days.deliverable: Event-sourced bank account service with CQRS, Kafka, and PostgreSQL

days.title: Cost Optimization & Cloud Economics

blocks.content: FinOps basics: tagging everything, cost allocation by team/service, setting budgets and alerts, regular cost reviews. How to estimate the cost of a system design before building it.

days.deliverable: Cost estimate for your microservices project: monthly AWS bill breakdown

days.title: Week 7 Review & Design Marathon

blocks.content: Design a ticket booking system (like BookMyShow): seat selection with concurrency (optimistic locking vs pessimistic locking), waiting room for popular events, payment timeout handling, inventory consistency.

days.deliverable: Instagram + Uber + Ticket Booking system design docs

weeks.title: Week 8 — Interview Prep & Capstone
weeks.goal: Consolidate everything. Practice system design interviews under time pressure. Build a capstone project that demonstrates your skills.

days.title: System Design Interview Framework Drill

blocks.content: Self-critique both designs: Did you clarify requirements first? Did you do back-of-envelope math? Did you justify your database choices? Did you discuss tradeoffs? Identify your weak spots.

days.deliverable: Payment system + Google Maps timed design docs with self-critique

days.title: System Design Practice — Day 2

blocks.content: Compare your designs with published solutions (ByteByteGo, Alex Xu's books). Note the differences. Are yours missing key components? Are you over-engineering anywhere?

days.deliverable: 3 timed system design docs with comparison notes against published solutions

days.title: Capstone Project — Design Phase

blocks.content: Set up the project: monorepo structure, shared Docker Compose for local dev, Terraform modules for infrastructure, GitHub Actions workflow. Run the skeleton services locally.

days.deliverable: Complete technical design document + project skeleton

days.title: Capstone — Core Services

blocks.content: Add async processing: SQS or Kafka for background work. For URL shortener: analytics event processing. For notifications: delivery workers (email/push). For Twitter: feed fan-out workers.

days.deliverable: Core service + data layer + async processing working end-to-end

days.title: Capstone — Scale & Resilience

blocks.content: Chaos testing: kill a service instance (does it recover?), slow down the database (does the circuit breaker trip?), fill the queue (does backpressure work?), overload with requests (does rate limiting kick in?).

days.deliverable: Load-tested, resilient capstone with monitoring and chaos test results

days.title: Capstone — Deploy & Document

blocks.content: Write a README worthy of a portfolio piece: what it is, why the tech choices, architecture diagram, how to run locally, how to deploy, performance benchmarks, what you'd improve with more time.

days.deliverable: Fully deployed capstone with architecture docs and portfolio-ready README

days.title: Rapid-Fire System Design Day

blocks.content: 30-min speed round: Design a job scheduler (like cron at scale). Exactly-once execution, missed job handling, distributed locking, priority queues, multi-tenant isolation.

days.deliverable: 4 speed-round system design docs (concise, 1-2 pages each)

days.title: Knowledge Gaps & Weak Spots

blocks.content: Find a friend or use Pramp / interviewing.io for a mock system design interview. Or record yourself solving a problem and watch the replay. Identify verbal tics, gaps in explanation, places where you hand-wave.

days.deliverable: Self-assessment with scores + study plan for remaining weak spots

days.title: Mock Interviews & Communication

blocks.content: Review both mocks. Score yourself on: requirement gathering, estimation, architecture clarity, depth of knowledge, tradeoff discussion, communication. Write down exactly what you need to improve for the remaining 2 days.

days.deliverable: 2 mock interview recordings/notes with self-assessment scores

days.title: Final Review — Everything Consolidated

blocks.content: Review your capstone project and be ready to discuss it in detail. Interviewers love hearing about projects where you made real architectural decisions. Prepare a 3-minute walkthrough of your architecture.

days.deliverable: Complete cheat sheet set + 3-minute capstone architecture pitch

days.title: Day 60 — Graduation & What's Next

blocks.content: You've put in 240 hours of focused study. Review how far you've come from Day 1. You now have a mental model for: cloud infrastructure, distributed systems theory, microservices architecture, deployment pipelines, system design interviews. That's a massive achievement.

days.deliverable: Portfolio-ready GitHub + continued learning plan + celebration 🎉
